content:
Every month I look at what my AI tooling costs and wonder if I’m overpaying. This month I actually checked, and the answer was stranger than I expected: the cheaper model cost me several times more.
I run a small pipeline that reads through large amounts of unstructured text and returns clean, structured records, working through it one chunk at a time. The reading is the expensive part, and I’d been doing it on Claude Opus. Opus is good and not the cheapest option, so I wired in a lower-priced model, Qwen3.7-Max on OpenRouter, fed both the same chunks, and watched.
The first thing that happened was that the cheaper model wouldn’t finish. Twice it took the whole job down, and when I traced it the fault was mine, not the model’s. A function I’d written long ago assumed the service would always return well-formed data and never checked. The new provider occasionally returned a plain error message instead, my code tried to parse it as structured data, and the run died. The flaw had sat there harmless for months because nothing had ever taken the path that reached it. I added a guard and a retry, and it ran.
I patched it and ran both cleanly. That’s when the cost came in: on the same input, each chunk ran about $0.13 to $0.20 on the cheaper model against about $0.03 to $0.06 on Opus, roughly four times more. Most of the gap came from two things, and neither is on a pricing page.
The first was wordiness. The cheaper model wrote far more to do the same job, about nine times the output, wrapping every answer in commentary I hadn’t asked for. You pay for what a model writes, and it wrote a great deal more.
The second was caching. The block of instructions and context I send with every chunk is long, around a hundred thousand tokens. Opus keeps it in a prompt cache, paying once to store it and then a small fraction to read it back each chunk after. The cheaper model, on the path I’d wired, got none of that and paid full price for the whole block every time, which accounted for most of its per-chunk cost. That isn’t a limit of the model; it has its own cache. I simply hadn’t rebuilt that piece for a new provider. A cheaper model, it turns out, doesn’t come with the optimizations you’d already built around the expensive one.
The one place the cheaper model kept pace was the quality of the output. I compared them blind, ten chunks each with the labels stripped, scoring faithfulness and usefulness on a two-point scale. The results came out nearly even, the cheaper model slightly ahead on usefulness and slightly behind on faithfulness. I trust those scores the least of anything here: the grader was from the same family as one of the two models, and the cheaper model’s answers were long enough to give themselves away. Comparable quality is worth something; it just wasn’t enough to offset four times the cost and ten times the wait.
So I stayed with the model I’d started on. Even if I’d closed the caching gap, the cheaper one wrote more, ran far slower, and had made me fix an old crash just to start. The number on the rate card told me almost nothing about the real bill. How much a model writes, and whether it can reuse the work you’ve already done, decided it, and I couldn’t see either until I ran my own load and read what it charged.
Fillip Kosorukov is a solo developer building data and AI tools. More at fillipkosorukov.net.
